problem_statement:
Showing progress in console is broken

Easy fix.... but let's note it here.
test_patch:
diff --git a/iminuit/test/testdescribe.py b/iminuit/test/testdescribe.py
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..d1b00e02d
--- /dev/null
+++ b/iminuit/test/testdescribe.py
@@ -0,0 +1,57 @@
+#describe needs its own separate test
+from nose.tools import *
+from iminuit.util import describe
+
+#simple function
+def f(x,y):
+    pass
+
+def test_simple():
+    assert_equal(describe(f,True), ['x','y'])
+
+#test bound method
+class A:
+    def f(self,x,y):
+        pass
+
+def test_bound():
+    a=A()
+    assert_equal(describe(a.f,True),['x','y'])
+
+#unbound method
+def test_unbound():
+    assert_equal(describe(A.f,True),['self','x','y'])
+
+#faking func code
+class Func_Code:
+    def __init__(self,varname):
+        self.co_varnames=varname
+        self.co_argcount=len(varname)
+
+#test __call__
+class Func1:
+    def __init__(self):
+        pass
+    def __call__(self,x,y):
+        return (x-2.)**2 + (y-5.)**2 + 10
+
+def test_call():
+    f1 = Func1()
+    assert_equal(describe(f1,True),['x','y'])
+
+#fake func
+class Func2:
+    def __init__(self):
+        self.func_code = Func_Code(['x','y'])
+    def __call__(self,*arg):
+        return (arg[0]-2.)**2 + (arg[1]-5.)**2 + 10
+
+def test_fakefunc():
+    f2 = Func2()
+    assert_equal(describe(f2,True),['x','y'])
+
+#builtin (parsing doc)
+def test_builtin():
+    assert_equal(describe(min),['iterable','key'])
+
+#any good way to test cython builtin call?

problem_statement:
Ambiguous contours output

When using the contours function with subtract_min=True and plotting using matplotlib's contours function, I get multiple lines that extend beyond the sigma (bound) of 1.0 that I thought I set.
hints_text:
The Z with `subtract_min=True` is value of the function away from minimum. To interpret this as how many sigma: each line of the countour is n^2*up where n is number of sigma.

http://wwwasdoc.web.cern.ch/wwwasdoc/minuit/node7.html

The automatic bound is from hesse which assumes parabolic error. If your profile is not exactly parabolic then ... just enlarge your bound.

If you are gettting weird image try using

`contour(x,y,z, levels=[1,4,9])` for chi^2 and `contour(x,y,z, levels=[0.5,2,4.5])` for likelihood


see #57

diff --git a/src/iminuit/_minuit.py b/src/iminuit/_minuit.py
index e7132c3b9..fc488ea4f 100644
--- a/src/iminuit/_minuit.py
+++ b/src/iminuit/_minuit.py
@@ -7,6 +7,7 @@
     MnMigrad,
     MnMinos,
     MnPrint,
+    MnSimplex,
     MnStrategy,
     MnUserParameterState,
     FunctionMinimum,
@@ -100,9 +101,13 @@ def errordef(self, value):
     def tol(self):
         """Tolerance for convergence.
 
-        The main convergence criteria of MINUIT is ``edm < edm_max``, where ``edm_max`` is
-        calculated as ``edm_max = 0.002 * tol * errordef`` and EDM is the *estimated distance
-        to minimum*, as described in the `MINUIT paper`_.
+        The main convergence criteria of MINUIT is ``edm < edm_max``, where ``edm_max``
+        is calculated as ``edm_max = 0.002 * tol * errordef`` in case of the MIGRAD
+        algorithm and as ``edm_max = tol * errordef`` in case of the SIMPLEX algorithm.
+        EDM stands for *estimated distance to minimum*, which is described in the
+        `MINUIT paper`_. The EDM criterion is well suited for statistical cost functions,
+        since it stops the minimization when parameter improvements become small
+        compared to parameter uncertainties.
         """
         return self._tolerance
 
@@ -510,7 +515,7 @@ def reset(self):
         return self  # return self for method chaining and to autodisplay current state
 
     def migrad(self, ncall=None, precision=None, iterate=5):
-        """Run MIGRAD.
+        """Run MnMigrad from the Minuit2 library.

problem_statement:
Feature: add scan

In some very hard cases, I found that a brute force grid search in pyminuit helped Migrad.

https://code.google.com/p/pyminuit/wiki/FunctionReference

Is there any work done to implement this in iminuit? If not, I could give it a try. Something like the scipy version, but with the same interface.

http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.brute.html
hints_text:
An alternative would be to embed the Scipy function and write a thin wrapper around it, but I don't know if licenses allow it. Pyminuit is GPL, so definitely not compatible.


Implementing Scan is not that hard it will just wrap around Minuit's scan. If you want to give it a try see migrad in the pyx file.


Oh... PyMinuit Scan is not Minuit Scan. Still it's not hard to write and see migrad if you want to make one.


@Dapid Do you want to give this a try?


I can give it a try.

I think the best approach is to use Scipy if it is present, with a fallback to a home cooked version if not. I will see how they do it.


I'd prefer `iminuit` stay independent from `scipy`.

As long as it's pure-python code like `scipy/optimize/optimize.py` we can just bundle a copy in `iminuit/extern/scipy_optimize.py` though  ... no need to spend time re-coding what's there already.

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v0.14.0/scipy/optimize/optimize.py#L2374


I also needed the PyMinuit scan function and found it missing from iminuit. I don't know if you have something smarter in mind, but here is a quick thing that I cooked up to do that job. First, a generator function to produce iterable N-dimension grid points using the arg format that scan() used:

```
def scangrid(*args, **kwargs):
    """
    Generator function which returns lists of parameter (name, value)
    2-tuples via yields (i.e. in a for-expression).

    The arguments are 4-tuples of (name, numpts, low, high) for each
    parameter; the generator iterations will then visit each of the
    lattice points in a grid with num points between low..high in each
    param.
    """
    vec = kwargs.get("vec", [])
    if args:
        # print "@", len(args) #, args
        var, npts, low, high = args[0]
        for val in np.linspace(low, high, npts):
            newargs = args[1:]
            newvec = vec + [(var, val)]
            newkwargs = kwargs
            newkwargs["vec"] = newvec
            for i in scangrid(*newargs, **newkwargs):
                yield i
            # TODO: in Python3 this can replace the extra for-loop above
            # yield from scan(*newargs, **newkwargs):
    else:
        yield vec
```

And then an example of it being used to find a brute force minimum:

```
def fcn(a,b,c):
    "Some function, i.e. Minuit.fcn"
    return a**2 + b**2 + c**2

# Find the minimum value of fcn(x) in the grid. Can maybe be done in cool one-liner ways
scanbest = (None, None)
gen = scangrid(("a", nscan, amin, amax), ("b", nscan, bmin, bmax), ("c", nscan, cmin, cmax))
for p in gen:
    x = fcn(**dict(p))
    if scanbest[1] is None or x < scanbest[1]:
        scanbest = (p, x)

print "BEST FROM SCAN = ", scanbest
```

From this, dict(scanbest[0]) can be used as starting point arg to the iminuit.Minuit constructor. Seems to work. This could all be bundled into a new method on Minuit if you wanted -- I didn't do it that way since it needs to be done in the Cython definition of the Minuit class, rather than be "appended" to it as a post-hoc method.

Is this any use? It'd be nice to not have to copy this code around to every place where I need to do a simple grid scan before gradient descent!


I think @agbuckley volunteered to contribute this feature to iminuit.

@piti118 - Could you please have a look at his last comment and say if you want this in iminuit and give feedback on API / implementation?


Removing v1.3 milestone. Not many users will use this, and there's no PR, so it shouldn't hold up a release given that we have a ton of good new stuff thanks to @HDembinski .

problem_statement:
fixing parameters

Hi,
it would be great to have the possibility of fixing parameters like

``` python
m=iminuit.Minuit(f)
m.fixed["x"] = True
```

a bit like pyminuit

Thanks for your great work,
marco
hints_text:
+1
I would like this feature, to prevent having to construct a function for each combination of parameters I want to float.

I get this error when trying to set limits to prevent floating. 
ValueError: limit for parameter Po218_N0 is invalid. (0.0, 0.0) 

I really like lmfit-py method of handleing this via Parameters class. 
Also is there no way to set only a low or high limit?


@piti118 What do you think?


@marcocamma @bungernut Sorry that this got lost ... I agree a better interface to fix / free parameters is needed in iminuit.

My understanding based on the [Minuit class API docs](http://iminuit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#minuit) is that it **is** possible to fix parameters via the `fix_` keywords on construction: `m = Minuit(f, fix_x=True)`. It's even possible to interact with those flags via the `fitarg` member:

``` python
>>> from iminuit import Minuit
>>> def f(x, y, z):
...         return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2
... 
>>> m = Minuit(f, pedantic=False)
>>> m.fitarg
{'fix_z': False, 'y': 0.0, 'z': 0.0, 'fix_y': False, 'error_x': 1.0, 'error_z': 1.0, 'limit_x': None, 'error_y': 1.0, 'limit_y': None, 'fix_x': False, 'limit_z': None, 'x': 0.0}
>>> m.fitarg['fix_x'] = True
>>> m.fitarg['fix_x']
True
```

I didn't look yet if this actually works and if this is covered by tests.
I also don't know yet if this is related to #163 or independent.

At the very least this feature should be documented in a high-level Sphinx docs section.
Probably it would also be possible to improve the API, i.e. introduce a Parameter class like `lmfit` or `Sherpa` or `Astropy` has it and expose it as an attribute on `Minuit` that makes it easy to set / get values / errors / fix flag, instead of having to go via `fitarg` and `fix_` strings.

Let's wait for @piti118 to comment first.

I'm not sure if someone will have the time to implement this though ... help needed!


One side limit is supported for quite a while. 

We could add method that wraps around fitarg. That should be easy enough.


It does not look like the suggested method m.fitarg["fix_x"] = True is working as shown by the code below:

``` python
from __future__ import print_function
import iminuit
import numpy as np

print("Iminuit version",iminuit.__version__)

def generateData(N=100,order=2,err=0.1):
  x = np.arange(N)
  coefs = np.random.random(order+1)
  y = np.polyval(coefs,x)+np.random.normal(scale=err,size=N)
  return coefs,x,y,err

def func(x,pars):
  return np.polyval(pars,x)
f1 = lambda x,a0,a1: a1*x+a0
f2 = lambda x,a0,a1,a2: a2*x**2+a1*x+a0
f3 = lambda x,a0,a1,a2,a3: a3*x**3+a2*x**2+a1*x+a0

class GenericChi2:
  def __init__(self, f, x, y,e=1.):
    self.f = f
    self.x = x; self.y=y; self.e=e
    args = iminuit.describe(f)#extract function signature
    self.func_code = iminuit.Struct(
      co_varnames = args[1:],#dock off independent param
      co_argcount = len(args)-1
    )
  def __call__(self, *arg):
    f = ( self.f(self.x,*arg)-self.y )/self.e
    return np.sum(f**2)

def doFit(N=100,order=2):
  # generate data
  right_values,x,y,e = generateData(N,order)
  right_values = right_values[::-1]
  # get fitting function
  f = globals()["f%d"%order]
  pars_dict = {}
  for i in range(order+1):
    pars_dict["error_a%d" % i] = 0.01
    pars_dict["a%d" % i] = 0.5
  im = iminuit.Minuit( GenericChi2(f,x,y,e),errordef=1,**pars_dict )
  im.migrad()
  p = im.values.keys(); p.sort()
  for i,pi in enumerate(p):
    print("Par %s, best fit %.4f +/- %.4f, exact value %.4f" % 
      (pi,im.values[pi],im.errors[pi],right_values[i]))


  im.fitarg["fix_a0"]=True
  im.fitarg["a0"]=0

  print("Is a0 fixed: %s"%im.is_fixed("a0"))
  im.migrad()
  p = im.values.keys(); p.sort()
  for i,pi in enumerate(p):
    print("Par %s, best fit %.4f +/- %.4f, exact value %.4f" % 
      (pi,im.values[pi],im.errors[pi],right_values[i]))

  return im

doFit()
```

Hi,
this is somewhat related.

Let's say I have a fit with two parameters p1 and p2, and I instance Minuit with p1 fixed to 1 and p2 free to vary.

How can I then change the value which p1 is fixed to, for example to 10? This doesn't work:

minuit.values['p1'] = 10

because (I guess) the internal minuit object is not updated, so the new fit still shows p1 fixed to 1.

+cdef setParameterState(MnUserParameterStatePtr state, object parameters, dict fitarg):
+    """Construct parameter state from user input.
+
+    Caller is responsible for cleaning up the pointer.
+    """
+    cdef double inf = float("infinity")
+    cdef double val
+    cdef double err
+    cdef double lb
+    cdef double ub
+    for i, pname in enumerate(parameters):
+        val = fitarg[pname]
+        err = fitarg['error_' + pname]
+        state.Add(pname, val, err)
+
+        lim = fitarg['limit_' + pname]
+        if lim is not None:
+            lb = -inf if lim[0] is None else lim[0]
+            ub = inf if lim[1] is None else lim[1]
+            if lb >= ub:
+                raise ValueError(
+                    'limit for parameter %s is invalid. %r' % (pname, (lb, ub)))
+            if lb == -inf and ub == inf:
+                pass
+            elif ub == inf:
+                state.SetLowerLimit(i, lb)
+            elif lb == -inf:
+                state.SetUpperLimit(i, ub)
+            else:
+                state.SetLimits(i, lb, ub)
+            #need to set value again
+            #taking care of internal/external transformation
+            state.SetValue(i, val)
+            state.SetError(i, err)
+
+        if fitarg['fix_' + pname]:
+            state.Fix(i)
+
+
+cdef auto_frontend():
+    """Determine frontend automatically.
+
+    Use HTML frontend in IPython sessions and console frontend otherwise.
+    """
+    try:
+        __IPYTHON__
+        from iminuit.frontends.html import HtmlFrontend
+        return HtmlFrontend()
+    except NameError:
+        from iminuit.frontends.console import ConsoleFrontend
+        return ConsoleFrontend()
+
+
+cdef check_extra_args(parameters, kwd):
+    """Check keyword arguments to find unwanted/typo keyword arguments"""
+    fixed_param = set('fix_' + p for p in parameters)
+    limit_param = set('limit_' + p for p in parameters)
+    error_param = set('error_' + p for p in parameters)
+    for k in kwd.keys():
+        if k not in parameters and \
+                        k not in fixed_param and \
+                        k not in limit_param and \
+                        k not in error_param:
+            raise RuntimeError(
+                ('Cannot understand keyword %s. May be a typo?\n'
+                 'The parameters are %r') % (k, parameters))
+
+
+# Helper classes
+cdef class BasicView:
+    """Dict-like view of parameter state.
+
+    Derived classes need to implement methods _set and _get to access
+    specific properties of the parameter state."""
+    cdef object _minuit
+    cdef MnUserParameterStatePtr _state
+
+    def __init__(self, minuit):
+        self._minuit = minuit
+
+    def __iter__(self):
+        return self._minuit.pos2var.__iter__()
+
+    def __len__(self):
+        return len(self._minuit.pos2var)
+
+    def keys(self):
+        return [k for k in self]
+
+    def items(self):
+        return [(name, self._get(k)) for (k, name) in enumerate(self)]
+
+    def values(self):
+        return [self._get(k) for k in range(len(self))]
+
+    def __getitem__(self, key):
+        cdef int i = key if isinstance(key, (int, long)) else self._minuit.var2pos[key]
+        return self._get(i)
+
+    def __setitem__(self, key, value):
+        cdef int i = key if isinstance(key, (int, long)) else self._minuit.var2pos[key]
+        self._set(i, value)
+
+    def copy(self):
+        return dict(self)
+
+    def __str__(self):
+        s = "%s{" % self.__class__.__name__
+        items = [" '{0}'={1}".format(k, v) for (k, v) in self.items()]
+        s += ",\n".join(items)
+        s += "\n}"
+        return s
+
+
+cdef class ArgsView:
+    """List-like view of parameter values."""
+    cdef object _minuit
+    cdef MnUserParameterStatePtr _state
+
+    def __init__(self, minuit):
+        self._minuit = minuit
+
+    def __len__(self):
+        return len(self._minuit.pos2var)
+
+    def __getitem__(self, int i):
+        if i < 0 or i >= len(self):
+            raise IndexError
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).Value()
+
+    def __setitem__(self, int i, double value):
+        if i < 0 or i >= len(self):
+            raise IndexError
+        self._state.SetValue(i, value)
+
+    def copy(self):
+        return tuple(self)
+
+    def __str__(self):
+        return 'ArgsView[' + ', '.join(str(x) for x in self) + ']'
+
+
+cdef class ValueView(BasicView):
+    """Dict-like view of parameter values."""
+    def _get(self, unsigned int i):
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).Value()
+
+    def _set(self, unsigned int i, double value):
+        self._state.SetValue(i, value)
+
+
+cdef class ErrorView(BasicView):
+    """Dict-like view of parameter errors."""
+    def _get(self, unsigned int i):
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).Error()
+
+    def _set(self, unsigned int i, double value):
+        self._state.SetError(i, value)
+
+
+cdef class FixedView(BasicView):
+    """Dict-like view of whether parameters are fixed."""
+    def _get(self, unsigned int i):
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).IsFixed()
+
+    def _set(self, unsigned int i, bint fix):
+        if fix:
+            self._state.Fix(i)
+        else:
+            self._state.Release(i)
+

problem_statement:
strange initial value behavior

Upgrading from v1.0.7 to v1.0.9 caused fits that used to work to fail. I'm having trouble coming up with a simple test case to illustrate the problem, but one piece of mysterious behavior is that the initial parameter values change between the two versions in the case where there are limits. Consider the simple function:

``` python
def f(x,y,z):
    print "CALL", x, y, z
    return (x-2.)**2 + (y-3.)**2 + (z-4.)**2

m = Minuit(f, x=2., limit_x=(-1.e5, 1.e5))
m.migrad()
```

In v1.0.7, this results in:

```
**************************************************
*                     MIGRAD                     *
**************************************************

CALL 1.99999999999 0.0 0.0
CALL 2.00999999999 0.0 0.0
CALL 1.98999999999 0.0 0.0
CALL 2.00248975561 0.0 0.0
CALL 1.99751024437 0.0 0.0
CALL 1.99999999999 0.01 0.0
CALL 1.99999999999 -0.01 0.0
CALL 1.99999999999 0.00248975551057 0.0
CALL 1.99999999999 -0.00248975551057 0.0
CALL 1.99999999999 0.0 0.01
[...]
```

as you'd expect. However, in v1.0.9, this results in

```
**************************************************
*                     MIGRAD                     *
**************************************************

CALL 49999.5 0.0 0.0
CALL 49999.0 0.0 0.0
CALL 50000.0 0.0 0.0
CALL 49999.5 1.0 0.0
CALL 49999.5 -1.0 0.0
CALL 49999.5 0.0 1.0
CALL 49999.5 0.0 -1.0
======================================
CALL 2.00001501323 3.0 4.0
CALL 1.95001501322 3.0 4.0
CALL 2.05001501323 3.0 4.0
CALL 2.00001501323 3.1 4.0
CALL 2.00001501323 2.9 4.0
CALL 2.00001501323 3.01 4.0
CALL 2.00001501323 2.99 4.0
[...]
```

Initially, minuit uses a value of x=49999.5 even though x is explicitly set to 2!

Sorry this bug report is not more complete. I'll add more to it as I investigate further.
hints_text:
I have a guess why that is. There is an internal and external variable inside minuit. Some function takes internal and some take external... I think I have screwed it up somewhere.


Upon investigation, Minuit does not really support one-sided limit. When a parameter is constructed, it's either unbounded or bounded. The minimization algorithm only works for unbounded one. So the trick is to transform unbounded one bounded one is transformed to unbounded one with arctan or something similar which needs the bound both sides.

The current behavior is just plain wrong since the parameter is constructed as unbounded and bound is put in later so there will be no transformation there.

Some note on one-sided there.

> One sided limits: It is common that one needs to restrict a particular parameter only on one side---for example, to be non-negative. If the user decides to set limits, in this case there is a temptation to set the upper limit to some very large number. This is sure to cause major problems (see the manual). It would be better to set the limits so that the location of the minimum is roughly midway between the lower and upper limit. But limits should only be used when they are really needed---if Minuit does not drift into the parameter's illegal region, no limit is needed for that parameter.

see more:http://www-cdf.fnal.gov/physics/statistics/recommendations/minuit.html


I'll revert back to the old behavior then. Sorry no one sided limit. :(


Oh well. Sorry for suggesting one sided limits, thought it would be trivial!


I thought that too. But this really need to be fixed.

hints_text:
OK noted. This is an important feature. I want to make sure python3 support works first and I'll do this one.


Dear developers, 

in our project (professor.hepforge.org) we switched from pyminuit to iminuit entirely and are very happy with it. The above mentioned feature would benefit our project as we can often construct the gradient of the function we want to minimise. Cpu time of the minimisation currently limits the application to ~ 10 dimensional parameter spaces so it would be cool to go beyond that. 
Do you have an estimate on whether that MINUIT functionality will be part of iminuit anytime soon?

Thanks,
Holger

+def func3_grad(x, y):

+    dfdy = 2 * (y - 5.)
+    return [dfdx, dfdy]

 def func4(x, y, z):
     return 0.2 * (x - 2.) ** 2 + 0.1 * (y - 5.) ** 2 + 0.25 * (z - 7.) ** 2 + 10
 
 
+def func4_grad(x, y, z):
+    dfdx = 0.4 * (x - 2.)
+    dfdy = 0.2 * (y - 5.)
+    dfdz = 0.5 * (z - 7.)
+    return (dfdx, dfdy, dfdz)
+
+
 def func5(x, long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long, z):
     return (x ** 2) + (z ** 2) + long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long ** 2
 
 
+def func5_grad(x, long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long, z):
+    dfdx = 2 * x
+    dfdy = 2 * long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long
+    dfdz = 2 * z
+    return (dfdx, dfdy, dfdz)
+
+

problem_statement:
non-optional dependence on IPython

With the latest master build but not the latest release version from pip (v1.1.1), I find a non-optional dependence on IPython being installed. To reproduce, install iminuit into a fresh environment with minimal dependencies (I used `conda create -n testiminuit numpy cython`), install iminuit from master, and try the example:

``` python
>>> from iminuit import Minuit
>>> def f(x, y, z):
...     return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2
... 
>>> m = Minuit(f)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx", line 1304, in iminuit._libiminuit.Minuit._auto_frontend (iminuit/_libiminuit.cpp:19288)
NameError: name '__IPYTHON__' is not defined

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx", line 296, in iminuit._libiminuit.Minuit.__init__ (iminuit/_libiminuit.cpp:3048)
  File "iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx", line 1308, in iminuit._libiminuit.Minuit._auto_frontend (iminuit/_libiminuit.cpp:19377)
  File "/home/kyle/.conda/envs/testiminuit/lib/python3.4/site-packages/iminuit-1.1.1-py3.4-linux-x86_64.egg/iminuit/frontends/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    from .html import *
  File "/home/kyle/.conda/envs/testiminuit/lib/python3.4/site-packages/iminuit-1.1.1-py3.4-linux-x86_64.egg/iminuit/frontends/html.py", line 5, in <module>
    from IPython.core.display import display_html
ImportError: No module named 'IPython'
```

On python 2.7, I get the traceback

```
>>> m = Minuit(f)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx", line 296, in iminuit._libiminuit.Minuit.__init__ (iminuit/_libiminuit.cpp:3048)
  File "iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx", line 1308, in iminuit._libiminuit.Minuit._auto_frontend (iminuit/_libiminuit.cpp:19377)
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/iminuit/frontends/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/iminuit/frontends/html.py", line 5, in <module>
ImportError: No module named IPython.core.display
```

I discovered this when running travis tests, where I don't install IPython. Is it possible that the import statements need to be removed from `iminuit/frontends/__init__.py`? Looking your way @cdeil ;) ...

problem_statement:
Explain how to get n-sigma errors/covariance matrix in basic tutorial

After running migrad I get out the sigma=1 errors if I initially set errordef=1. However, I'd also like to access the sigma=2 errors without running migrad again (extremely slow!). In pyminuit I could simply run hesse setting up=4, but it seems the parameter up is not defined for iminuit. I cannot find the workaround anywhere in the documentation and would therefore like to ask here for a solution.
Thanks a lot,
Jette

Hi Christoph,

Thanks for looking into this.
Attached please find a minimum working example of code using pyminuit that I’d like to port to iminut.
The key is that I want to change the value of the parameter called errordef for iminuit (up for pyminuit) and see what that does to the estimated errors (rerunning hesse) without having to minimise the function all over again (as I typically work with very complex functions where rerunning migrad takes longer than just rerunning hesse).

Jette

import minuit as minuit
import time

def f(x,y,z):
return (x-1)**2+(y-2)**2+(z-3)**2

m = minuit.Minuit(f)
t1=time.clock()
m.migrad()
t2=time.clock()
print "time for migrad: %0.1e" %(t2-t1)
print m.up
print m.values
print m.errors
m.up = 0.25
t1=time.clock()
m.hesse()
t2=time.clock()
print "time for hesse: %0.1e" %(t2-t1)
print m.up
print m.values
print m.errors
t1=time.clock()
m.migrad()
t2=time.clock()
print "time for second migrad: %0.1e" %(t2-t1)

@jetteodder - You can get 1, 2, 3 sigma errors like this:
```
errors_1_sigma = 1 * m.np_errors()
errors_2_sigma = 2 * m.np_errors()
errors_3_sigma = 3 * m.np_errors()
```
No need to run MIGRAD or HESSE multiple times.

This was discussed at length by @HDembinski  and me in https://github.com/iminuit/iminuit/pull/215

problem_statement:
Analytical derivatives?

Is there a way to specify the analytical derivatives of the function to minimize?

For simple functions this speed up a lot the fitting, and it is a feature implemented in Minuit2.
hints_text:
This is the same feature request as #125.
As far as I know, this Minuit2 functionality hasn't been exposed in iminuit and no-one's working on it at the moment.
test_patch:
diff --git a/iminuit/tests/test_iminuit.py b/iminuit/tests/test_iminuit.py
index cc386b05c..e10f9ac65 100644
--- a/iminuit/tests/test_iminuit.py
+++ b/iminuit/tests/test_iminuit.py
@@ -33,14 +33,34 @@ def func3(x, y):
     return 0.2 * (x - 2.) ** 2 + (y - 5.) ** 2 + 10
 
 
+def func3_grad(x, y):
+    dfdx = 0.4 * (x - 2.)
+    dfdy = 2 * (y - 5.)
+    return [dfdx, dfdy]
+
+
 def func4(x, y, z):
     return 0.2 * (x - 2.) ** 2 + 0.1 * (y - 5.) ** 2 + 0.25 * (z - 7.) ** 2 + 10
 
 
+def func4_grad(x, y, z):
+    dfdx = 0.4 * (x - 2.)
+    dfdy = 0.2 * (y - 5.)
+    dfdz = 0.5 * (z - 7.)
+    return (dfdx, dfdy, dfdz)
+
+
 def func5(x, long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long, z):
     return (x ** 2) + (z ** 2) + long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long ** 2
 
 
+def func5_grad(x, long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long, z):
+    dfdx = 2 * x
+    dfdy = 2 * long_variable_name_really_long_why_does_it_has_to_be_this_long
+    dfdz = 2 * z
+    return (dfdx, dfdy, dfdz)
+
+
 def func6(x, m, s, A):
     return A / ((x - m) ** 2 + s ** 2)
 
@@ -137,6 +157,36 @@ def test_fix_param():
     assert 'z' not in fix_param
 
 
+def test_fix_param_with_gradient():
+    m = Minuit(func4, grad_fcn=func4_grad, print_level=0, pedantic=False)
+    m.migrad()
+    m.minos()
+    val = m.values
+    assert_allclose(val['x'], 2)
+    assert_allclose(val['y'], 5)
+    assert_allclose(val['z'], 7)
+    err = m.errors  # second derivative
+    assert_allclose(err['x'], 2.236067977354171)
+    assert_allclose(err['y'], 3.1622776602288)
+    assert_allclose(err['z'], 2)
+    m.print_all_minos()
+    # now fix z = 10
+    m = Minuit(func4, grad_fcn=func4_grad, print_level=-1, y=10., fix_y=True, pedantic=False)
+    m.migrad()
+    val = m.values
+    assert_allclose(val['x'], 2)
+    assert_allclose(val['y'], 10)
+    assert_allclose(val['z'], 7)
+    assert_allclose(m.fval, 10. + 2.5)
+    free_param = m.list_of_vary_param()
+    fix_param = m.list_of_fixed_param()
+    assert 'x' in free_param
+    assert 'x' not in fix_param
+    assert 'y' in fix_param
+    assert 'y' not in free_param
+    assert 'z' not in fix_param
+
+

problem_statement:
grad_fcn with parameters given in an array 

Adding the gradient option in iminuit.Minuit speeds-up a lot the minimization when numerous variables are used. Thank you for this recent improvement.
From what I've understood, the variable values at which the gradient will be calculated must be given explicitly in the gradient function: 
def func4_grad(x, y, z):
...
this is not possible when fitting lot's of variables (cases for which the gradient input is needed).
Is there a way to use an array as argument to the gradient function ? I tried but it did not work, I could not find the corresponding line code in the sources files. Something like:
def func4_grad(double[::1] my_variables):
...
Thanks !
hints_text:
I see that in PythonGradientFCN.h the grad function is called with a vector in Gradient:
```
Gradient(const std::vector<double>& x)
PyObject\* tuple = vector2tuple(x);
PyObject\* result = PyObject_Call(gradfcn,tuple,NULL);
```

But I can not find the link between this function and the grad_fcn function of _libiminuit.pyx which is provided by the user.
Can someone points the lines where it is done ?


Is iminuit still maintained ? If not it would be useful to mention it somewhere since it is an important parameter when one chooses an option or another. Thank you anyway for this work.

problem_statement:
[bug] minos minos sigma=2 gives one sigma error

When fixing sigma=2. to minos, the given errors are the same as not fixing it. So I bet they give the one sigma error.
hints_text:
@londumas - Thanks for the bug report!

For reference, the API docs for `Minuit.minos` are [here](http://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#iminuit.Minuit.minos) and the tests are in [iminuit/tests/test_iminuit.py](https://github.com/iminuit/iminuit/blob/master/iminuit/tests/test_iminuit.py).

Looks like there is no test that calls `minos` passing a `sigma`.
There's only one test passing `sigma`  to `mncontour`: `test_mncontour_sigma`.

@londumas - Can you contribute a test that shows the issue, i.e. runs a minimal example and has an assert statement at the end on what you expect, that currently fails.
That would be the first step, then the next one is to look at the implementation and fix it.

I don't have time, sorry.
Help welcome!


ok, I am writing it. interestingly, it works in 1.1.1


@londumas - Thank you!


Here is a working example. Could you have a look ?

The idea is to try to get the error on a Gaussian filled with numpy.rand.normal

[test_error_minos.txt](https://github.com/iminuit/iminuit/files/356626/test_error_minos.txt)


I have a way simplier example here:
```
import iminuit

def f(x):
    return x**2

m= iminuit.Minuit(f)
m.migrad()
==> error = +/- 1
m.minos()
==> error = +/- 2
m.minos(sigma=2)
==> error = +/- 3
m.minos(sigma=3)
```

What more can I do to have this issue fixed ?


The bug might be in iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx,

self.pyfcn.set_up(oldup * sigma * sigma) at line 569

could you have a look, it could be cool to fix that.


something might have happen here:

https://github.com/iminuit/iminuit/commit/cf718b74c24ed79e96b186d4cec556b2b1ceac8d

because there is no problem iniminuit==1.2 

@chrisburr could you look what changed you have made that broke the minos sigma errors ?

+def test_num_call():
+    class Func:

+    assert m.get_num_call_fcn() == func.ncall

+    m.set_errordef(4)
+    m.hesse()
+    assert_allclose(m.errors["x"], 2)

problem_statement:
pixellation issues in contour plots in highly covariant variables

I am using iminuit to do some fitting with very strong covariance between variables and I'm getting a lot of contour plots that look like this by default:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3793500/27530162-0a475536-5a8a-11e7-9a97-e22e6dee28cb.png)

This should show a thin ellipse. It doesn't due to the pixellation of the underlying data
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3793500/27531702-357f5004-5a90-11e7-800b-85d6b7ecd8f3.png)

If I zoom in with nbin=40 and bound=0.5, the true covariance is clearly well-behaved.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3793500/27531488-5e2c4e68-5a8f-11e7-8e28-3a627142e217.png)
 
I would suggest having an odd number as the default for 'bins' as this at least ensures a central peak. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3793500/27531057-ca5721be-5a8d-11e7-813d-f8b88fd119f4.png)

Having the option to add the raster image (via imshow) under the contours might also be useful option, and perhaps should be considered as the default.
hints_text:
@johnsmorgan - Apologies for not responding here.

I'm not sure what exactly you mean here or how to devise a simple test case.

Are you just suggesting to change the default for `bins=30` in
http://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#iminuit.Minuit.draw_mnprofile
to e.g. `bins=31`? Would that help in your case?

I think I understand the issue. Using uneven bin number may improve the situation but does not solve the underlying problem. The way how the contour plot is made is wrong. I need to have a closer look into draw_mnprofile to fix this properly. The MINOS algorithm is able to draw exact contours. We should not use these brittle approximations.

@HDembinski - OK.

But there's a ton of methods to contour and parameters.

As always, my suggestion would be to mainly improve the docs, and refer them to scikit-image and MPL contouring, advertise these plot methods in iminuit only as quick-look.

And as always, I don't want to be responsible for a software that gives such dangerously wrong results.

problem_statement:
interface request: add np_vector, similar to np_matrix

```iminuit.Minuit``` already provides a nice convenience interface to get the full covariance matrix after the fit: ```iminuit.Minuit.np_matrix()```. Unfortunately, there is no equivalent method for getting the parameter vector. For consistency reasons and because I personally need this all the time, I would like to see a method ```iminuit.Minuit.np_vector()``` that returns a vector of the fitted parameters in the same order as `np_matrix` is returned.
hints_text:
I see that `Minuit.np.matrix` is basically a one-liner calling `np.array` on `Minuit.matrix`:
https://github.com/iminuit/iminuit/blob/fc5cc5d89f15921fbd78b27396eda22a5f99b487/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx#L649
```
np_matrix = np.array(minuit.matrix())
```

I looked around a bit, and it seems no equivalent of `Minuit.matrix` exists, that exposes an ordered parameter list. One currently has to use this to turn the `Minuit.values` dict into a list with the parameters in the right order?
```
[m.values[name] for name in m.list_of_vary_param()]
```
Or did I just miss that property and it exists already?

@HDembinski - So basically what you're proposing is a property that returns this, no?
```
np.array(m.values[name] for name in m.list_of_vary_param())
```

---

In principle I'm +1 to add such a convenience(s).

But given that anything we add should remain there forever, I would prefer us to be careful / conservative with additions and to have someone review and propose a full addition of more Python-based properties (using tuples / lists / dicts, or instead changing to OrderedDict to avoid duplicating properties just to get sorted lists), and then which properties we really want to re-expose using Numpy arrays instead of just letting the caller pass an existing list or tuple into `np.array`. Note that there's different variants like "all parameters" or only the "varying parameters" or ... it would also be reasonable to add 10 new Numpy array convenience properties.

The alternative to adding conveniences is to improve the docs. Probably some mix of those two options would be idea.

@HDembinski @piti118 @alexpearce or anyone - what do you propose we add? Anyone willing to send a pull request (maybe wait for a few days to get opinions here)?

I would be happy to send a PR once we converge on the interface. :)

I think a better name than `np_vector()` would be `np_values()`, and instead of `np_matrix()` there should be `np_covariance()`. I would keep `np_matrix()`, but add a deprecation warning, suggesting the use of `np_covariance()`. For convenience and consistency, there should also be a `np_errors()`.

I will try to motivate this. The new method names are chosen to mirror the standard interface to get the fit results, the properties `values`, `errors`, and `covariance`, which have clear names.

Why add this interface? There are people like me, who do further numerical computation with the fitted parameters and their covariance matrix. To motivate this, I need to do some exposition of a typical workflow I have (and many others I am sure).

I do numerical error propagation a lot. In my library [pyik](https://github.com/HDembinski/pyik) there is a general function to do this:
```
from pyik.numpyext import propagate_covariance

# do some fitting here, get vector fit_values and matrix covariance_matrix

def mapping_function(fit_values):
     # ... do something with fit_values, produce some output vector

propagated_covariance = propagate_covariance(mapping_function, fit_values, covariance_matrix)
```
How is this useful? You need it when you want to draw an error band around your fitted model. The `mapping_function` in this case evaluates the model at various `x` positions and returns the result `y=f(x)` as a function of the fitted parameters.

But drawing error bands is just one reason. For people who work with numpy a lot, it is simply more natural to get the fit result and the covariance matrix as numpy ndarrays. It scales better when you need to change the number of fitted parameters a lot, for example, when you fit a polynomial of variable order. That is a common case for me. For code that works with a vector of fitted parameters, taking out the fixed parameters is not useful, it breaks the error propagation example shown above

Therefore, `np_values()` should be implemented like this:

```
def np_values(self):
    return numpy.fromiter(self.values[key] for key in self.parameters, dtype=float, count=len(self.parameters))
```

The method returns the full vector of model parameters, not distinguishing between fixed and floating parameters. Similarly, `np_covariance()` should be implemented like this:

```
def np_covariance(self):
    n = len(self.parameters)
    m = numpy.empty(n, n)
    for i1, k1 in enumerate(self.parameters):
        for i2, k2 in enumerate(self.parameters):
           m[i1, i2] = self.covariance[(k1, k2)]
    return m
```


> For people who work with numpy a lot, it is simply more natural to get the fit result and the covariance matrix as numpy ndarrays. It scales better when you need to change the number of fitted parameters a lot, ...

Agreed that supporting interaction (getting and setting) with Numpy arrays should be a goal, i.e. to make that as convenient as possible. But Minuit first and foremost has a Python tuple / dict / list interface. Usually interacting with Numpy arrays instead of tuples is possible with one-liners. I'm +100 to document that better (the code snippets from this issue discussion should be added to docstrings or even a section "Numpy array interface of iminuit.Minuit" in the high-level docs.

But after looking a bit more closely at the current API http://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html I'm not sure I agree with your suggestions, @HDembinski . In the end it's a matter of taste how much and how exactly to expose a numpy array interface, so hopefully others (e.g. @piti118 @alexpearce) will chime in as well.

I'm -1 on deprecating [Minuit.matrix](http://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#iminuit.Minuit.matrix). I'm using it in years old scripts and I think many others are too, so deprecating it is an inconvenience. I also think it's very nice to have access to the matrix in covariance or correlation form, as well as with fixed parameters in or out. All of those things are needed / useful, and the current a`matrix` and `np_matrix` are exactly how I would put it. I realise it partly duplicates other things (like `covariance`), which isn't nice, but note that this issue is proposing to add more duplication / convenience properties for info that is already exposed.

For me, the thing that sticks out as most confusing is http://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#iminuit.Minuit.args , I think improving the docstring there and saying how it compares to `.values` would be most valuable. See
https://github.com/iminuit/iminuit/blob/fc5cc5d89f15921fbd78b27396eda22a5f99b487/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx#L379
Is it equivalent to `self.values[key] for key in self.parameters`? Then we could just write
```
def np_values(self):
    return np.array(self.args)
```
or really adding this has very little value, because the caller can just do `np.array(m.args)`.

Finally, it's not clear to me if we can't just change from dict -> OrderedDict everywhere, and have parameters in order, and then `np.array(m.values.values())` and `np.array(m.errors.values())` would work, and probably some similar expression for `merrors`?

So bottom line: I would prefer if we first add a short section or page "Numpy array interface of iminuit.Minuit" to the docs with examples how to do all the things you want to do, and how they can be done currently. I think providing that would be a huge benefit to many users. Adding one-line convenience methods and properties can be done as well, but that would probably always remain incomplete (e.g. what about "merrors"? where can I pass numpy arrays to Minuit methods or assign numpy arrays to properties? I don't know the answer to that question).

Finally, just to be clear: we're only talking about adding Numpy array support in the interface that goes through the Python tuple interface? I.e. there are absolutely no performance improvements in any of those additions, right? Those would require changing `iminuit.Minuit` to internally store things as Numpy arrays, which would be a huge change, and in the end unlikely to matter for performance, at least as long as you don't have e.g. 10,000 or 100,000 parameters, i.e. a regime where as I understand Minuit isn't the optimiser of choice anyways.

@cdeil and I discussed this a bit offline. My argument is that most people nowadays use numpy, so it would be nice to have a convenient interface with clear names for those users. Even if you could get the same via one-liners, an one-liner that I have to type *in every single script* is still annoying.

`Minuit.args` and `Minuit.matrix()` are not good names. Imagine you read a script where these appear, it is not clear what these do from the name alone. "Readability counts" (Zen of Python). We owe it to the numpy users that we do not thread them as second-class citizens. My suggestion is to mimic the normal interface `Minuit.values` and `Minuit.covariance` and make equivalent numpy versions `Minuit.np_values()` and `Minuit.np_covariance()`. This leads to much more readable code, and there is a clear correspondence between the non-numpy and numpy interfaces.

We already discussed the option of changing everything to OrderedDict, it is a good idea, but it would not work for the covariance matrix.

I am ok with not deprecating `matrix()`, I may have been overzealous here.

I am fine with adding Numpy array support that goes through the Python tuple interface, as performance is not a concern. However, I would like to have the interface in such a way that it is possible to optimize this in the future, if someone wants to put in the effort.
patch:
diff --git a/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx b/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx
index f388f94c1..74ac1e80a 100644
--- a/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx
+++ b/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx
@@ -564,14 +564,12 @@ cdef class Minuit:
         """Error or correlation matrix in tuple or tuples format."""
         if self.last_upst is NULL:
             raise RuntimeError("Run migrad/hesse first")
-        if not skip_fixed:
-            raise RuntimeError('skip_fixed=False is not supported')
         if not self.last_upst.HasCovariance():
             raise RuntimeError(
                 "Covariance is not valid. May be the last Hesse call failed?")
 
         cdef MnUserCovariance cov = self.last_upst.Covariance()
-        params = self.list_of_vary_param()
+        params = self.list_of_vary_param() if skip_fixed else self.parameters
         if correlation:
             ret = tuple(
                 tuple(cov.get(iv1, iv2) / sqrt(cov.get(iv1, iv1) * cov.get(iv2, iv2))
@@ -603,13 +601,77 @@ cdef class Minuit:
         """Error or correlation matrix in numpy array format.
 
         The name of this function was chosen to be analogous to :meth:`matrix`,
-        it returns the same information in a different format.
+        it returns the same information in a different format. For
+        documentation on the arguments, please see :meth:`matrix`.
 
-        Note that a ``numpy.ndarray`` is returned, not a ``numpy.matrix``
+        **Returns:**
+
+            2D ``numpy.ndarray`` of shape (N,N) (not a ``numpy.matrix``).
         """
         import numpy as np
         matrix = self.matrix(correlation=correlation, skip_fixed=skip_fixed)
-        return np.array(matrix, dtype=np.float64)
+        return np.array(matrix, dtype=np.double)
+
+    def np_values(self):
+        """Parameter values in numpy array format.
+
+        Fixed parameters are included, the order follows :attr:`parameters`.
+
+        **Returns:**
+
+            ``numpy.ndarray`` of shape (N,).
+         """
+        import numpy as np
+        return np.array(self.args, dtype=np.double)
+
+    def np_errors(self):
+        """Hesse parameter errors in numpy array format.
+
+        Fixed parameters are included, the order follows :attr:`parameters`.
+
+        **Returns:**
+
+            ``numpy.ndarray`` of shape (N,).
+        """
+        import numpy as np
+        a = np.empty(len(self.parameters), dtype=np.double)
+        for i, k in enumerate(self.parameters):
+            a[i] = self.errors[k]
+        return a
+
+    def np_merrors(self):
+        """Minos parameter errors in numpy array format.
+
+        Fixed parameters are included, the order follows :attr:`parameters`.
+
+        The format of the produced array follows matplotlib conventions, as
+        in ``matplotlib.pyplot.errorbar``. The shape is (2, N) for N
+        parameters. The first row represents the downward error as a positive
+        offset from the center. Likewise, the second row represents the
+        upward error as a positive offset from the center.
+
+        **Returns:**
+
+            ``numpy.ndarray`` of shape (2, N).
+        """
+        import numpy as np
+        # array format follows matplotlib conventions, see pyplot.errorbar
+        a = np.empty((2, len(self.parameters)), dtype=np.double)
+        for i, k in enumerate(self.parameters):
+            a[0, i] = -self.merrors[(k, -1.0)]
+            a[1, i] = self.merrors[(k, 1.0)]
+        return a
+
+    def np_covariance(self):
+        """Covariance matrix in numpy array format.
+
+        Fixed parameters are included, the order follows :attr:`parameters`.
+
+        **Returns:**
+
+            ``numpy.ndarray`` of shape (N,N) (not a ``numpy.matrix``).
+        """
+        return self.np_matrix(correlation=False, skip_fixed=False)
 
     def is_fixed(self, vname):
         """Check if variable *vname* is (initially) fixed"""

problem_statement:
python setup.py test is broken

I see this error locally:
```
$ python setup.py test
running test
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "setup.py", line 140, in <module>
    'build_ext': SmartBuildExt,
  File "/Users/deil/Library/Python/3.6/lib/python/site-packages/setuptools/__init__.py", line 129, in setup
    return distutils.core.setup(**attrs)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/distutils/core.py", line 148, in setup
    dist.run_commands()
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/distutils/dist.py", line 955, in run_commands
    self.run_command(cmd)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/distutils/dist.py", line 974, in run_command
    cmd_obj.run()
  File "/Users/deil/Library/Python/3.6/lib/python/site-packages/setuptools/command/test.py", line 216, in run
    cmd = ' '.join(self._argv)
TypeError: sequence item 2: expected str instance, bool found
```

I think this was there all the time, and we just didn't invocate the tests like that.

I think copy & pasting the latest code from here to update our setup.py would fix the issue:
https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html#manual-integration

Or we switch to this:
https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html#integrating-with-setuptools-python-setup-py-test-pytest-runner

I haven't looked at the pros and cons of the different ways to run tests in a while, so I'm not sure.
@HDembinski - Any opinions?
If not, we could keep as-is and I just do the update in `setup.py` to fix this.
hints_text:
@cdeil I looked into `setup.py` and apparently we went for 'manual-integration', it is just broken. I tried a few things to get it working again, and got some improvement, but then another failure. I don't really understand the error and how all this works. I am starting to find setuptools really annoying...

My preferred solution would be to drop setup.py and use cmake to build this project. This would solve several of the issues I have with the current build. I don't know how that would work with pypi integration?

@HDembinski - The `setup.py` has to remain, it's what `pip` uses, the standard how Python packages are built. It is possible to call into other build systems from `setup.py`. I don't know if calling into `cmake` would mean an extra dependency on `cmake`, or if `cmake` generates something that just works without extra dependency on all platforms we target (like what Cython does). 

I think most or all people agree that setup.py isn't a great build system for C++.  But I don't think we should try to replace it, even if isn't possible somehow. Never touch a running system.

I'll try to find some time next week to look at how we run tests and find a solution to the issue mentioned above.

pybind11 has an example, where setup.py calls cmake to compile the code.
https://github.com/pybind/cmake_example/blob/master/setup.py
I think this is the way to go.
Yes, this would add an extra dependency on cmake, but cmake is a standard in the C++ world now, so it is something that people likely have installed already. It looks like you can even install cmake with PyPI
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/cmake

I think "never touch a running system" only make sense when
a) you cannot thoroughly test the system (but we can, we have CI for linux and windows now, and perhaps soon for OSX)
b) you don't understand the system (it is a build system, and a simple one)

If you want to stick to setup.py, then let me know how we can set it up in such a way, that Minuit is build into a separate shared library that is linked to _libiminuit.so. I looked into the setuptools docu and could not find a way to compile a C++ shared libary and link another shared library to the first, all within setup.py.
If you can do all that with setuptools, ok, then I have no practical reason to propose a move to cmake.

I think a main reason why people like `iminuit` is that it has zero dependencies and is easy to install. IMO the concern is mainly distribution, it's not clear if install really still works smoothly if we need cmake. E.g. I have a Raspberry Pi, who knows is `pip install cmake` there works. And what if I did `sudo apt-get install cmake` there? -> an extra moving part is an extra moving part and some users will have problems.

Users don't care if Minuit2 is in a separate shared lib, no? The benefits I see of moving to cmake are 1. simplification of our setup.py and 2. faster build, because cmake uses multi-core. The drawback is the extra dependency / moving part. We can discuss the change if you want, in a new issue, but to me it doesn't seem like a good move.

Whether the setuptools build can be improved: I don't know. There must be Python projects that have C++ that don't call out to cmake or other build systems. Maybe scikit-learn? This question could also be a good one for stackoverflow if it doesn't exist already.

Ok, you convinced me, no cmake then and we try to find out how to build two c++ libs that link to each other without cmake. And we still need to figure out how to fix this issue. My attempt failed and I would have to dig deeper. Perhaps someone with more experience in setuptools could give it a try?
patch:
diff --git a/setup.py b/setup.py
index 79466c92a..f8deb4bfe 100644
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -39,19 +39,20 @@ def build_extensions(self):

+    user_options = [('pytest-args=', 'a', "Arguments to pass to pytest")]

         TestCommand.initialize_options(self)
         # self.pytest_args = '--pyargs iminuit'
         # self.pytest_args = ['--strict', '--verbose', '--tb=long', 'tests']
-        self.pytest_args = []
-        self.test_suite = True
+        self.pytest_args = ''
 
     def run_tests(self):
         # import here, cause outside the eggs aren't loaded
         import pytest
-        errno = pytest.main(self.pytest_args)
+        import shlex
+        errno = pytest.main(shlex.split(self.pytest_args))
+        del sys.exitfunc # needed to avoid a bug caused by IPython's exitfunc
         sys.exit(errno)

problem_statement:
change current parameter state

There seems to be no way to change the current parameter state in an existing Minuit instance.

Changes made to `m.fitarg[name]` or `m.values[name]` are ignored. This is extremely confusing, because these values should not be settable, if they are read-only.

Changes in `m.fitarg`, `m.values`, and `m.errors` should actually update the internal state of Minuit.

Related to #163 #105
patch:
diff --git a/iminuit/Minuit2.pxi b/iminuit/Minuit2.pxi
index 5ba9359ad..e95cfbbfe 100644
--- a/iminuit/Minuit2.pxi
+++ b/iminuit/Minuit2.pxi
@@ -102,13 +102,13 @@ cdef extern from "Minuit2/MnUserParameterState.h":
     cdef cppclass MnUserParameterState:
         MnUserParameterState()
         MnUserParameterState(MnUserParameterState mpst)
-        vector[double] Params()
+        operator=(MnUserParameterState)
+
         void Add(char*name, double val, double err)
         void Add(char*name, double val, double err, double, double)
         void Add(char*, double)
 
         vector[MinuitParameter] MinuitParameters()
-        #MnUserParameters parameters()
         MnUserCovariance Covariance()
         MnGlobalCorrelationCoeff GlobalCC()
 
@@ -116,22 +116,21 @@ cdef extern from "Minuit2/MnUserParameterState.h":
         double Edm()
         unsigned int NFcn()
 
-        void Fix(char*)
-        void Release(char*)
-        void SetValue(char*, double)
-        void SetError(char*, double)
-        void SetLimits(char*, double, double)
-        void SetUpperLimit(char*, double)
-        void SetLowerLimit(char*, double)
-        void RemoveLimits(char*)
+        const MinuitParameter& Parameter(unsigned int)
+
+        void Fix(unsigned int)
+        void Release(unsigned int)
+        void SetValue(unsigned int, double)
+        void SetError(unsigned int, double)
+        void SetLimits(unsigned int, double, double)
+        void SetUpperLimit(unsigned int, double)
+        void SetLowerLimit(unsigned int, double)
+        void RemoveLimits(unsigned int)
 
         bint IsValid()
         bint HasCovariance()
         bint HasGlobalCC()
 
-        double Value(char*)
-        double Error(char*)
-
         unsigned int Index(char*)
         char*Name(unsigned int)
 
diff --git a/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx b/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx
index 3bb16ec8e..d16b9b345 100644
--- a/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx
+++ b/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx
@@ -24,10 +24,184 @@ np.import_array()
 
 __all__ = ['Minuit']
 
-# Our wrappers
+# Pointer types
 ctypedef FCNGradientBase* FCNGradientBasePtr
 ctypedef IMinuitMixin* IMinuitMixinPtr
 ctypedef PythonGradientFCN* PythonGradientFCNPtr
+ctypedef MnUserParameterState* MnUserParameterStatePtr
+
+# Helper functions
+cdef setParameterState(MnUserParameterStatePtr state, object parameters, dict fitarg):
+    """Construct parameter state from user input.
+
+    Caller is responsible for cleaning up the pointer.
+    """
+    cdef double inf = float("infinity")
+    cdef double val
+    cdef double err
+    cdef double lb
+    cdef double ub
+    for i, pname in enumerate(parameters):
+        val = fitarg[pname]
+        err = fitarg['error_' + pname]
+        state.Add(pname, val, err)
+
+        lim = fitarg['limit_' + pname]
+        if lim is not None:
+            lb = -inf if lim[0] is None else lim[0]
+            ub = inf if lim[1] is None else lim[1]
+            if lb >= ub:
+                raise ValueError(
+                    'limit for parameter %s is invalid. %r' % (pname, (lb, ub)))
+            if lb == -inf and ub == inf:
+                pass
+            elif ub == inf:
+                state.SetLowerLimit(i, lb)
+            elif lb == -inf:
+                state.SetUpperLimit(i, ub)
+            else:
+                state.SetLimits(i, lb, ub)
+            #need to set value again
+            #taking care of internal/external transformation
+            state.SetValue(i, val)
+            state.SetError(i, err)
+
+        if fitarg['fix_' + pname]:
+            state.Fix(i)
+
+
+cdef auto_frontend():
+    """Determine frontend automatically.
+
+    Use HTML frontend in IPython sessions and console frontend otherwise.
+    """
+    try:
+        __IPYTHON__
+        from iminuit.frontends.html import HtmlFrontend
+        return HtmlFrontend()
+    except NameError:
+        from iminuit.frontends.console import ConsoleFrontend
+        return ConsoleFrontend()
+
+
+cdef check_extra_args(parameters, kwd):
+    """Check keyword arguments to find unwanted/typo keyword arguments"""
+    fixed_param = set('fix_' + p for p in parameters)
+    limit_param = set('limit_' + p for p in parameters)
+    error_param = set('error_' + p for p in parameters)
+    for k in kwd.keys():
+        if k not in parameters and \
+                        k not in fixed_param and \
+                        k not in limit_param and \
+                        k not in error_param:
+            raise RuntimeError(
+                ('Cannot understand keyword %s. May be a typo?\n'
+                 'The parameters are %r') % (k, parameters))
+
+
+# Helper classes
+cdef class BasicView:
+    """Dict-like view of parameter state.
+
+    Derived classes need to implement methods _set and _get to access
+    specific properties of the parameter state."""
+    cdef object _minuit
+    cdef MnUserParameterStatePtr _state
+
+    def __init__(self, minuit):
+        self._minuit = minuit
+
+    def __iter__(self):
+        return self._minuit.pos2var.__iter__()
+
+    def __len__(self):
+        return len(self._minuit.pos2var)
+
+    def keys(self):
+        return [k for k in self]
+
+    def items(self):
+        return [(name, self._get(k)) for (k, name) in enumerate(self)]
+
+    def values(self):
+        return [self._get(k) for k in range(len(self))]
+
+    def __getitem__(self, key):
+        cdef int i = key if isinstance(key, (int, long)) else self._minuit.var2pos[key]
+        return self._get(i)
+
+    def __setitem__(self, key, value):
+        cdef int i = key if isinstance(key, (int, long)) else self._minuit.var2pos[key]
+        self._set(i, value)
+
+    def copy(self):
+        return dict(self)
+
+    def __str__(self):
+        s = "%s{" % self.__class__.__name__
+        items = [" '{0}'={1}".format(k, v) for (k, v) in self.items()]
+        s += ",\n".join(items)
+        s += "\n}"
+        return s
+
+
+cdef class ArgsView:
+    """List-like view of parameter values."""
+    cdef object _minuit
+    cdef MnUserParameterStatePtr _state
+
+    def __init__(self, minuit):
+        self._minuit = minuit
+
+    def __len__(self):
+        return len(self._minuit.pos2var)
+
+    def __getitem__(self, int i):
+        if i < 0 or i >= len(self):
+            raise IndexError
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).Value()
+
+    def __setitem__(self, int i, double value):
+        if i < 0 or i >= len(self):
+            raise IndexError
+        self._state.SetValue(i, value)
+
+    def copy(self):
+        return tuple(self)
+
+    def __str__(self):
+        return 'ArgsView[' + ', '.join(str(x) for x in self) + ']'
+
+
+cdef class ValueView(BasicView):
+    """Dict-like view of parameter values."""
+    def _get(self, unsigned int i):
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).Value()
+
+    def _set(self, unsigned int i, double value):
+        self._state.SetValue(i, value)
+
+
+cdef class ErrorView(BasicView):
+    """Dict-like view of parameter errors."""
+    def _get(self, unsigned int i):
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).Error()
+
+    def _set(self, unsigned int i, double value):
+        self._state.SetError(i, value)
+
+
+cdef class FixedView(BasicView):
+    """Dict-like view of whether parameters are fixed."""
+    def _get(self, unsigned int i):
+        return self._state.Parameter(i).IsFixed()
+
+    def _set(self, unsigned int i, bint fix):
+        if fix:
+            self._state.Fix(i)
+        else:
+            self._state.Release(i)
+

problem_statement:
Why console printout `import re`?

With iminuit master and the example from the docs page I get this output.
Why is there a printout of `import re`!?

@HDembinski - Do you know / see the same? Or should I investigate?

```
In [3]: %paste
from iminuit import Minuit
def f(x, y, z):
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2
m = Minuit(f)
m.migrad()
print(m.values)  # {'x': 2,'y': 3,'z': 4}
print(m.errors)  # {'x': 1,'y': 1,'z': 1}

## -- End pasted text --
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: errordef is not given. Default to 1.
  import re
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: Parameter x does not have initial value. Assume 0.
  import re
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: Parameter x is floating but does not have initial step size. Assume 1.
  import re
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: Parameter y does not have initial value. Assume 0.
  import re
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: Parameter y is floating but does not have initial step size. Assume 1.
  import re
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: Parameter z does not have initial value. Assume 0.
  import re
/Users/deil/software/anaconda3/envs/iminuit/bin/ipython:4: InitialParamWarning: Parameter z is floating but does not have initial step size. Assume 1.
  import re
{'x': 2.0000000000047327, 'y': 3.000000000007099, 'z': 4.000000000009465}
{'x': 1.0000000000000409, 'y': 1.0000000000002762, 'z': 0.9999999999998322}
```
hints_text:
Hi, I can confirm this with IPython, but it does not appear in the normal Python interpreter. Also, it does not appear when the function is a lambda, only when it is a real function.

Minimal code:
```
from iminuit import Minuit
def f(x): return x** 2
Minuit(f)
```

I think this is a bug in `warnings` or in `IPython`.

I did some reading how the `warnings` module works. Whenever a warning is raised, the warning is formatted with the standard implementation of this function.
`warnings.formatwarning(message, category, filename, lineno[, line])`
See https://docs.python.org/2/library/warnings.html#warnings.formatwarning
When `line` is not given (which we don't do), it uses the line specified by filename and lineno.

We call `warn` from Cython, and it gets confused. It uses the IPython instance as filename, and some lineno which seems arbitrary (it is certainly not the line where `warn` was called in _libiminuit.pyx`). In my case, it is lineno 4. Since IPython is a script with the content
```
#!/usr/bin/python

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re
import sys

from IPython import start_ipython

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(start_ipython())
```
Line number 4 is just `import re`. Now we know at least where it comes from.

@HDembinski - Thanks for figuring this out.

If I understand correctly in #233 you monkey-patch  `warnings.formatwarning`.
This fixes the issue, but it probably also leads to the side effect that just `import iminuit` might lead to changes in warnings formatting and head-scratching for others.

I found only one post on this issue, proposing a different hack:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2647128/emitting-cythonic-warnings

Ask on the Cython mailing list for advice / how others have handled the issue?

@cdeil If you don't like my solution, could you take over then? I already spend a good deal of time figuring out what is going on. I agree that there must be a better solution, this one may have side effects, just like you said.

I've asked on https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/cython-users
I think the mailing list is moderator approved, but the message should appear soon.
Let's wait a few days for answers there and then decide what change to make in iminuit.

I think the answer given in https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2647128/emitting-cythonic-warnings
could work for us, that you found. I will give it a quick try. The solution in this case has no side effects, as far as I can see. In any case, the problem is in Cython and we can only work around it.



https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/cython-users/hLNi1y4-Htg

Thank you for the comprehensive posting, this looks great. I hope we get a reply.

This also seems related: https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/1755
The stack frames in Cython are faked, but only when an exception is raised. This is not done when a warning is raised, which is why warnings behave so strangely.

@HDembinski - See https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/11188 and
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/cython-users/hLNi1y4-Htg

My suggestion would be to just wait and see if / what changes are made in ipython or Cython, and only if the problem is still there say in a month or two, make a change in iminuit.


Ok, I tried to play around with the code a bit and found no solution. I will wait.

Removing v1.3 milestone. It's been like this for years and no user complained.
Probably this issue will be resolved within the next months by the iminuit or Cython folks.

+            assert w[i].lineno == 866
